title: Assessing the Anthropogenic and Climate Change Drivers in Shaping Landscape
body: Assessing the Anthropogenic and Climate Change Drivers in Shaping Landscape 2023 Thesis Ajoy Karmakar NA

title: Impact of Fire on Carbon Flux in North-Western Himalaya
body: Fig. 1 — Study area: North-Western Himalaya Forests play a crucial role as major carbon reservoirs in terrestrial ecosystems, primarily acting as carbon sinks by taking in atmospheric carbon through photosynthesis. However, during extreme disturbances such as forest fires, they shift from being carbon sinks to becoming carbon sources. Ongoing climate change has significantly altered the stability of forest ecosystems, increasing their susceptibility to extreme disturbances. Among these disturbances, forest fires have become a major contributor to carbon loss, especially in fire-prone areas such as the North-Western Himalaya (NWH). This research examined variations in carbon flux caused by fire events across this ecologically fragile region using the Functionally Assembled Terrestrial Ecosystem Simulator (FATES) integrated within the Community Land Model Version 5 (CLM 5.0.3), which is a process-based Dynamic Global Vegetation Model (DGVM). The simulations were conducted for the year 2016 to analyze monthly patterns of fire occurrence, carbon emissions, and forest productivity, with particular emphasis on Gross Primary Productivity (GPP). Fig. 2 — Monthly GPP simulated by CLM5-FATES for 2016 The model successfully represented the seasonal pattern of GPP, showing reduced values in winter months (January–February), followed by a steady rise during spring, reaching a peak in late summer (August–September), and then declining towards the end of the year. A comparison with eddy covariance (EC) observations from flux tower locations at Barkot and Haldwani showed strong agreement, with R² values exceeding 0.7, indicating that the model effectively captured ecosystem-level productivity. Despite this, the simulation results showed no fire occurrences during the study period, with the burned area recorded as zero. This inconsistency is likely due to the use of coarse spatial resolution, the dominance of small-scale fire events in the region, and the limited representation of human-induced fires in the model setup. Fig. 3 — Observed fire occurrence points in the study region (2016) Observations indicate that small but frequent fire incidents occur during the summer months, particularly in April and May, leading to notable carbon emissions. These emissions were mainly concentrated in the southeastern part of the study region. Fig. 4 — Net Biome Productivity (NBP) across the North-Western Himalaya The findings highlight the intricate relationship between fire activity, human influence, and ecosystem productivity in the NWH. The study demonstrates the effectiveness of CLM5-FATES modeling frameworks in capturing seasonal ecosystem dynamics, while also pointing out their limitations in representing disturbance-related carbon fluxes, especially when anthropogenic influences are underrepresented. Further improvements in the model, including the integration of high-resolution fire datasets and better representation of human-driven fire processes, are necessary for more accurate assessment of forest carbon dynamics in densely populated and disturbance-prone regions.

title: Carbon and Ecohydrological Dynamics of High Altitude Grasslands
body: Fig. 1 — Grasslands in Indian Himalayan Region High-altitude Himalayan grasslands are fragile yet vital ecosystems that regulate regional climate, water availability, and carbon storage, while sustaining biodiversity and pastoral livelihoods. Their sensitivity to environmental change stems from extreme elevation, harsh climatic conditions, and limited ecological resilience, making them particularly vulnerable to climate change and human pressures. Systematic monitoring of carbon and hydrological fluxes is therefore essential: carbon fluxes reveal whether these landscapes act as sinks or sources of greenhouse gases, shaping global carbon budgets and climate feedbacks, while hydrological fluxes illuminate soil moisture dynamics, evapotranspiration, and downstream water supply critical to river systems and communities. By analyzing ecohydrological variability, scientists gain insights into ecosystem functioning, resilience, and sustainability, advancing both fundamental understanding and informing conservation strategies and policy frameworks aimed at safeguarding these unique high-altitude environments for the future.

title: Analysis of Cyclone induced vulnerability of coastal districts of West Bengal Odisha using MCDM
body: Back to Homepage Analysis of Cyclone induced vulnerability of coastal districts of West Bengal Odisha using MCDM Sudikin Pramanik , Aparajita Ghosh , Ajoy Karmakar , Avijnan Das , Sameer Mahapatra , Mithesh Kumar Jha November, 2023 Conference --> Fig. 1 — Super Cyclone AMPHAN making landfall (Mid May 2020) [Source: IMD] Coastal districts of West Bengal and Odisha frequently experience severe cyclonic storms originating from the Bay of Bengal, causing significant loss of life, infrastructure damage, and economic disruption. Districts including North 24 Parganas, South 24 Parganas, East Medinipur, Balasore, Bhadrak, Kendrapara, Jagatsinghpur, Puri, and Ganjam are particularly vulnerable because of their low elevation, long coastlines, dense population, and dependence on agriculture and coastal resources. The present study focused on assessing cyclone-induced vulnerability of these coastal districts using an integrated AHP–TOPSIS Multi-Criteria Decision-Making approach. Several physical and socio-economic indicators were considered, including cyclone frequency, cyclone intensity, percentage of area below 5 m elevation, population density, annual rainfall, Human Development Index (HDI), non-working population, coastline length, land use/land cover (LULC), and mangrove cover. The Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) was used to assign weights to the selected criteria, while TOPSIS was applied to rank districts according to their vulnerability levels. The combined approach provided a systematic framework for identifying areas with higher exposure and lower adaptive capacity to cyclone hazards. The results identified North 24 Parganas as the most vulnerable district due to high cyclone frequency and intensity, low-lying terrain, dense population, and extensive agricultural land. South 24 Parganas ranked second because of its difficult deltaic topography, long coastline, frequent cyclone exposure, and relatively low socio-economic conditions. Balasore emerged as the third most vulnerable district because of higher cyclone occurrence, high rainfall, and dominance of cropland areas. East Medinipur ranked fourth, influenced by its long shoreline, high cyclone exposure, and comparatively low HDI. Bhadrak, Puri, Ganjam, Kendrapara, and Jagatsinghpur also showed moderate to high vulnerability due to coastal exposure, agricultural dependency, dense settlements, and socio-economic limitations. Puri was particularly exposed because of its extensive coastline, while Ganjam showed increased risk associated with dense population and built-up areas. Fig. 2 — Spatial distribution of cyclone-induced vulnerability across coastal districts derived using the AHP-TOPSIS approach. The study demonstrates that both environmental and socio-economic factors strongly influence cyclone vulnerability in eastern coastal India. The AHP–TOPSIS framework effectively identified high-risk districts and can support disaster management planning, climate adaptation strategies, and coastal resilience development. The results emphasize the need for improved infrastructure, sustainable coastal management, and region-specific mitigation measures to reduce future cyclone impacts.

title: Forest PFT Classification using Remote Sensing Machine Learning
body: Back to Homepage Forest PFT Classification using Remote Sensing Machine Learning Ajoy Karmakar June- July, 2024 Conference --> The concept of Plant Functional Types (PFTs) provides a robust framework for grouping vegetation based on shared structural, phenological, and physiological characteristics, as well as their functional responses to environmental conditions. Unlike biome-level classifications, which represent broad vegetation zones such as deserts, savannahs, and deciduous forests, PFTs enable a finer and more process-oriented representation of ecosystem functioning. This becomes particularly important in ecosystem modeling, where species-level parameterization is often constrained by the lack of detailed trait information. In the present study, an advanced methodology was developed to classify forest PFTs in the North-Western Himalayan (NWH) region using Fourier Transformation of time-series NDVI data coupled with a Random Forest machine learning algorithm. The Fourier decomposition allowed extraction of key temporal features such as amplitude, phase, and mean vegetation dynamics, which are critical indicators of vegetation phenology and functional behavior. Fig. 1 — Animated representation of vegetation indices including True Color Composite (TCC), False Color Composite (FCC), NDVI phase, amplitude, Fourier transformed outputs, and mean NDVI. The extracted Fourier features were used as inputs to the Random Forest classifier, enabling the differentiation of vegetation types based on their temporal signatures rather than solely on spectral characteristics. The classification results demonstrated strong performance, with an overall accuracy of 0.8257 and a Cohen’s Kappa coefficient of 0.7943, indicating substantial agreement with ground truth observations. Fig. 2 — Example of Fourier transformed NDVI components highlighting temporal vegetation dynamics. In contrast, the MODIS PFT product (MOD12Q1) showed significantly lower performance in representing the actual vegetation distribution of the region. The product yielded an overall accuracy of 0.3329 and a Cohen’s Kappa of 0.2126, indicating poor agreement with field observations. Notably, in regions such as the Shiwalik range, Dehradun, and Rishikesh, areas dominated by deciduous forests were incorrectly classified as evergreen forests, highlighting limitations in the global classification scheme when applied to complex mountainous terrains. The discrepancies observed suggest that global PFT products may not adequately capture regional heterogeneity, particularly in ecologically sensitive and topographically diverse areas like the NWH. The proposed methodology demonstrates that integrating time-series analysis with machine learning provides a more reliable and region-specific classification framework. Fig. 3 — Final Plant Functional Type (PFT) classification map derived using Random Forest and Fourier-transformed NDVI features. Furthermore, the study indicates that a single-level Fourier decomposition captures significant phenological variation across vegetation types. However, areas with low and stable NDVI signals remain challenging to classify, suggesting that incorporating higher-order harmonics or additional temporal features could further improve classification accuracy. Overall, the results emphasize the need for regionally calibrated PFT datasets for improved ecosystem modeling. The integration of ground truth data with machine learning algorithms such as Random Forest proves to be a powerful approach for generating accurate and scalable PFT maps, which are essential for understanding ecosystem sensitivity and carbon dynamics under changing climatic conditions.

title: Coupling atmospheric and hydrodynamic model for rapid forecast of surface runoff
body: Back to Homepage Coupling atmospheric and hydrodynamic model for rapid forecast of surface runoff Avijnan Das , Nilay Dey , Ajoy Karmakar , Sayantan Majumder Nov 30, 2024 Conference --> The coupling between the atmospheric model (WRF–ARW) and the hydrodynamic model (HEC–RAS) proved highly effective. By integrating meteorological inputs from Metgrid, static boundary conditions from Geogrid, and terrain information from the Digital Elevation Model (DEM), the system was able to simulate rainfall forecasts with remarkable accuracy. These forecasts were then combined with land use/land cover (LULC) data and SCS curve number–based infiltration parameters to drive the hydrodynamic simulations. As a result, the coupled framework successfully predicted the devastating flood that occurred in the Chaliyar river basin, Kerala, between 10–15 August 2018, a full 15 days in advance. This achievement highlights the robustness of the methodology outlined in the flowchart. Fig. 1 — Methodology Flowchart The second major objective was to develop an Earth Engine–based interactive mapping platform. This tool allows users to visualize the spatial extent of flood inundation in a dynamic and user-friendly way. By linking the forecast outputs with historical flood maps and cloud-based visualization, the system provides an interactive interface where flood spread areas can be explored in detail. Fig. 2 — GEE Application At present, the entire forecasting and visualization pipeline can be updated on a 15-day cycle, ensuring that both the hydrological predictions and the interactive maps remain current and relevant for decision-making and disaster preparedness.
